answer:
In the Smṛti corpus, *atithyam* (hospitality) refers to the ritualized honoring of an *atithi*—typically an unexpected traveling Brahmin guest who arrives unannounced, especially during the *Vaiśvadeva* oblation (the daily ritual meal offering to all deities).[^1] [^2] [^3] An *atithi* is defined etymologically as one who comes without a fixed (*anitya*) time or prior arrangement: a weary traveler from afar, not a local resident, relative, or someone arriving for a specific purpose (e.g., social or business reasons), even if arriving when the wife or sacred fires are present.[^4] [^5] [^6] Hospitality entails providing a seat, water for washing feet (*pādya*), respectful words (*arghya* or pleasant inquiry), and food according to ability, without scrutiny of the guest's lineage, learning, or character—treating them as an embodiment of all deities.[^7] [^8] The guest must not be turned away hungry, especially if arriving at sunset or evening; neglect shatters their hopes, rendering the householder's offerings fruitless, sending ancestors to hell (or denying them food for 15 years), and dooming the householder to rebirth as a crow or similar.[^9] [^10] [^11]

Among householder (*gṛhastha*) duties, *atithyam* ranks as one of the *pañca-mahāyajñas* (five great sacrifices), performed daily alongside oblations to gods (*devayajña*, via *homa*), ancestors (*pitṛyajña*), beings (*bhūteyajña*, via *bali*), and Brahma (*brahmayajña*, via Vedic study).[^12] [^13] [^14] It is the "sacrifice to men" (*manuṣyayajña*), binding the householder to support the world, as all stages of life depend on the *gṛhastha*.[^15] Pārāśara lists it explicitly among six daily duties (twilight worship, *japa*, *homa*, deity/guest worship, *Vaiśvadeva*, and *atithi* hospitality).[^16] Viṣṇu equates the *atithi* to the "lord of the householder," superior even to Vedic study or *agnihotra* for attaining heaven.[^17] Neglect equates the householder to a corpse (breathing but dead), while fulfillment yields wealth, fame, long life, heaven, and ancestral satisfaction.[^18] [^19]

The *gṛhastha* āśrama is paramount, as it sustains society; *atithyam* ensures this by channeling merit to ancestors and gods via the guest's "mouth-fire."[^20] It must precede the householder's meal, after deities/ancestors/servants but before one's own eating.[^21]
